title: sample-accurate editor loops
description:
Current editor looping uses the HTMLAudioElement by polling currentTime and setting audio.currentTime back to loopStart when playback reaches loopEnd. That is useful but not guaranteed to be gapless or sample-accurate. The browser may seek, decode, and resume a few milliseconds late, which can make a 1-bar loop from beat 1 to beat 5 feel uneven even when beat markers are mathematically correct.

Context from the current implementation:
- Loop markers are stored as loopMarkers.startMs and loopMarkers.endMs in src/App.tsx.
- The scheduler now treats loop end as exclusive, so a note exactly on beat 5 should not be played in a beat 1 to beat 5 loop.
- The scheduler also looks across the loop boundary so beat 1 notes can be pre-scheduled before the audio wraps.
- The remaining possible hiccup is inherent to repeatedly seeking an <audio> element, not necessarily a beat grid bug.

Goal:
Add a Web Audio based playback path for editor loop mode so practice/edit loops can be smooth and clocked by the audio engine instead of repeated JS seeks.

Likely approach:
1. Keep HTML audio for basic playback/import behavior until Web Audio is proven.
2. For editor loop playback, fetch/decode the imported audio into an AudioBuffer.
3. Use AudioContext + AudioBufferSourceNode with source.loop = true, source.loopStart = loopStartMs / 1000, and source.loopEnd = loopEndMs / 1000.
4. Track playhead using the Web Audio clock, not audio.currentTime, while Web Audio playback is active.
5. Keep projectiles/note scheduling aligned to the same clock.
6. Support play, pause, seek, restart, loop marker changes, and cleanup when changing songs/beatmaps.
7. Fall back gracefully to current HTML audio if decode fails or no loop is active.

Design concerns:
- AudioBufferSourceNode is one-shot, so pause/resume and loop marker changes require creating a new source.
- Full-song decoding increases memory use, acceptable for local desktop/editor workflow but should be handled deliberately.
- Avoid duplicating playback state in fragile ways. Prefer a small playback engine module or hook, e.g. src/audio/useSongPlayback.ts or src/audio/webAudioPlayback.ts.
- Do not regress normal song playback, import cache URLs, or recording against the timeline.

Acceptance criteria:
- Beat 1 to beat 5 loop treats beat 5 as the exclusive endpoint.
- Loop playback is audibly smoother than repeated HTML audio seeks.
- Playhead stays synced during loop playback.
- Note/projectile scheduling remains correct across the loop boundary.
- TypeScript and production build pass.

title: better use for long notes
description: we need to improve everything about notes that need to be held down. first of all there needs to be some juice when playing them, should feel a bit like grinding with a skateboard if you catch my drift, sparks or something and a feeling of something being pressed down physically. then we need the ability to manually create long notes in the editor without using record. i feel like it should work by ctrl pressing and holding (to avoid confusion with selection marquee) to be able to drag it longer (snap should be respected). when hovering over the end of a long note we need to be able to drag it left or right to extend or shorten it.

title: ensure constant performance both during play and edit
description:
Performance hardening for both play and editor. Rhythm timing must remain stable under rendering and editor load, with no visible frame pacing issues or input/judgement drift.

Measure first, then optimize against explicit budgets. Current audit findings:
- The main scheduler polls every 10ms, scans and sorts the full beatmap, and updates React state, causing the full 1,300-line App component to rerender during playback.
- Editor playback also updates songTimeMs and timelineCenterMs every 10ms, rebuilding timeline rendering at up to 100Hz.
- Gamepad polling uses setInterval(16), which can drift and compete with rendering.
- Miss cleanup uses another interval and performs nested state updates.
- Timing is split between HTMLAudioElement.currentTime and performance.now(), so main-thread stalls can affect spawn timing and visual alignment even when judgement math is correct.

Proposed scope:
1. Add a lightweight development performance monitor: frame interval p50/p95/p99/max, long-task count and duration, scheduler lateness, input-to-handler latency where available, audio-clock versus visual-clock drift, active note count, and dropped-frame warnings. Keep telemetry buffered outside React and publish at low frequency.
2. Create deterministic stress fixtures for dense maps and large editor maps. Record baselines during play, editor playback, timeline zoom/drag, and recording. Test with CPU throttling as well as normal speed.
3. Establish budgets, initially: no long task over 50ms during active playback, p99 frame interval under 25ms at 60Hz, scheduler lateness under 10ms p99, and no accumulating clock drift. Tune after baseline data.
4. Separate the high-frequency playback clock and note scheduler from App React state. Use requestAnimationFrame for visuals and gamepad polling, refs/external state for transient values, indexed/sorted note traversal instead of scanning and sorting the whole map every tick, and low-frequency React updates only for visible UI readouts.
5. Memoize or split the editor and game surfaces so playhead updates do not rerender unrelated controls. Virtualize or window timeline notes and grid lines.
6. Add an automated browser performance scenario and document a repeatable profiling procedure using Chrome Performance and React Profiler.
7. Re-run production build, correctness tests, and performance scenarios with regression thresholds.

Related task 6dt is a focused audio-clock improvement for sample-accurate editor loops. It belongs to the same reliability effort and should share the playback clock abstraction and drift metrics, but it does not replace this task because xbh also covers render load, scheduling complexity, input polling, and editor frame pacing.

sources.content: we can not afford the smallest lag or the rhythm will be off in what the player needs to press. it will affec the ux greatly and also is bad for "health" / eyes. how can we harden this? do we need to measure first somehow?

selected and pending note retention in timeline LOD
remaining: automated browser stress fixture and regression thresholds

completion.summary: Validated normal play, editor playback, fit view, and recording with stable frame pacing, no dropped-frame threshold violations in final manual runs, negligible scheduler overhead, dense-map windowing, and clear live/off-screen hold rendering.

title: Package companion for one-click setup
description: Create a nontechnical companion distribution flow, prioritizing Windows and then other supported desktop platforms. Produce a self-contained signed installer or executable that requires no Node.js, npm, terminal, or environment variables. Use the centralized hosted Beat Fiend URL, provision or bundle checksum-verified media tools, launch securely on loopback, open and pair https://beatfiend.vercel.app automatically, provide clear startup/error/status feedback, support clean uninstall and updates, document the release process, and add a prominent download/run flow to the hosted app. Acceptance: a new user can download, launch, pair, and import YouTube audio using only standard GUI interactions; browser-only file upload remains available without installation; existing security boundaries remain intact.

title: revisit the name/brand
description: Beat Fiend is kind of nice. But I am worried its maybe got a bit of an "edgy" and adult connotation when i think this is an app that can also be sort of kid friendly. what do you think? ma ybe it is fine? or not? should we brainstorm some other (available) names?

sources.content: Beat Fiend is kind of nice. But I am worried its maybe got a bit of an "edgy" and adult connotation when i think this is an app that can also be sort of kid friendly. what do you think? ma ybe it is fine? or not? should we brainstorm some other (available) names?

Naming review completed 2026-07-19 (preliminary screen, not legal clearance).

Verdict:
- The concern is valid. "Beat Fiend" is memorable and energetic, but "fiend" also carries devil, wicked-person, and addict/drug associations. That makes it less naturally welcoming for a family-friendly creative tool.
- The exact name also has longstanding music uses, including Dan Curtin's 2009 Beatfiend release and other tracks/artist identities. No obvious exact-name rhythm app was found, but search uniqueness is weak.
- Recommendation: keep Beat Fiend as a working title if desired, but rename before a broader public launch. Do not rush into a weaker generic Beat + noun name.

Best initial shortlist:
1. GrooveSketch: friendliest and clearest fit for the draft/jam/refine workflow. Exact-name web screen found no obvious app/game collision; npm name appeared unused; groovesketch.com and groovesketch.app returned RDAP 404 (apparently unregistered at check time). Main risk: nearby MusicSketch app/name, and "sketch" can imply incompleteness.
2. Jamjectory: most ownable and product-specific, joining jamming with projectile trajectories. Exact-name screen found no obvious collision; npm appeared unused; jamjectory.com and jamjectory.app appeared unregistered. Main risk: pronunciation and spelling need user testing.
3. Jammetry: playful jam + geometry concept, fitting live input on a structured grid. Exact-name screen found no obvious product collision; npm and jammetry.app appeared open, but jammetry.com is registered. Main risk: spelling/pronunciation.
4. Pulsewright: polished craft-oriented backup. No obvious exact app collision and npm/.app appeared open, but pulsewright.com is registered.

Candidates screened out:
- Beatloom: existing music artist/catalog use; .com registered.
- Pulseplot: existing JS/Python library and npm package.
- Melodrift: exact AI melody app and active domains.
- TuneTrail: existing software/projects and registered domains.

Suggested next step:
Test GrooveSketch, Jamjectory, Jammetry, and Beat Fiend with 5-10 intended users. Ask what each name suggests, intended age/audience, how they spell it after hearing it, and which they would trust as a creative music tool. Then deeply screen the winner across trademark databases, app stores, Steam/itch, domains, and social handles before visual identity work.

Key sources:
- Merriam-Webster "fiend": https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/fiend
- Beatport Beatfiend release: https://www.beatport.com/release/beatfiend/153772
- Bandcamp Beatfiend release: https://dancurtin.bandcamp.com/album/beatfiend-2
- USPTO clearance guidance: https://www.uspto.gov/trademarks/search/comprehensive-clearance-search-similar-trademarks

Follow-up naming direction from product discussion:
- "GrooveSketch" can map directly onto the app's central seamless mode toggle.
- Groove = play/playtest mode, using a warm red-oriented identity.
- Sketch = edit/create mode, using a cool blue-oriented identity.
- This gives the name product meaning rather than being only decorative branding. The transition between modes can visually blend the two palettes, reinforcing that play and editing are one continuous workflow.

Follow-up screen for "JamLab" (preliminary, not legal clearance):
- Recommendation: do not pursue. The collision risk is high in the same broad music/software market.
- jamlab.app is registered and currently presents "Jam.Lab," an apparent browser-based workspace where bands write, record, mix, collaborate, and release music. This is a direct music-software overlap.
- M-Audio previously sold JamLab, a USB guitar interface bundled with computer music software; official/archived drivers and manuals remain searchable. Even if discontinued, it creates longstanding exact-name recognition in music technology.
- The JamLab at jamlabmusic.com is an active Toronto music school serving children and adults, adding exact-name overlap with the intended family-friendly audience.
- jamlab.com has been registered since 2004, jamlab.app since 2025, and jamlab.io is registered/for sale. No exact npm package was found.
- A quick web/trademark-index search did not establish a current exact US federal registration, but that does not resolve common-law rights, pending applications, other jurisdictions, or confusion. The direct marketplace collisions are enough to reject it before paying for formal clearance.

Online screen, compact play-word + workspace-word names (2026-07-19, preliminary only):
- BeatLab: reject. Active beatlab.app rhythm-training suite, exact Apple music app, Korg BeatLab hardware history, all major exact domains registered, npm occupied.
- SongLab: reject. Multiple exact Apple music apps plus US SONGLAB application serial 98804771 covering AI music SaaS; major domains registered.
- HitLab: reject. Established HITLAB music-technology company and exact domain.
- TuneLab: reject. Longstanding professional piano-tuning software on desktop/iOS/Android.
- PlayLab: reject/high risk. Crowded software and child-entertainment field with an exact US application.
- SongForge: reject. Direct kid-oriented music creation/DAW/game-software overlap.
- BeatForge: reject. Exact beat-production software and rhythm-game uses.
- JamForge: reject/high risk. Exact music-service search presence plus an active Unity framework.
- JamMap, JamGrid, and JamSketch: reject. Each has exact prior music-app/software use; JamMap also has registered .com and .app.
- BeatBench and BeatDesk: reject. Exact music-production/event-music software uses.
- JamDock: reject. Exact Alesis music hardware product.
- JamWorks: crowded exact software name; not attractive for clearance.
- JamBench and JamDraft had no obvious same-market exact collision in the quick web/app/package screen. Their .app domains appeared unregistered and npm exact names appeared unused, but both .com domains are registered. They remain brainstorming leads only, not cleared marks.
Conclusion: the intuitive Lab/Forge field is highly crowded. Current least-collided leads from this batch are JamBench and JamDraft, although neither communicates editing as immediately as JamLab.

Pad-name screen (preliminary, 2026-07-19):
- Pad is conceptually strong because it means both a musical performance pad and a sketch/edit surface.
- JamPad: reject. Exact Google Play beat-maker app, existing JamPad music software/platforms, occupied npm, .com, and .app.
- BeatPad: reject. Multiple exact drum-machine/beat-maker apps; .com and .app registered.
- SongPad: reject. Active exact songwriting software; .com and .app registered.
- TrackPad: reject. Generic Apple term plus exact MIDI/drum software uses; domains registered.
- PlayPad and RiffPad also have registered exact .com/.app domains and existing uses.
- HitPad had no obvious exact same-market result in the first screen and hitpad.app appeared unregistered, but hitpad.com is registered and "hit" introduces aggressive connotations. Not currently recommended.
Conclusion: Pad is an excellent semantic suffix but the obvious music/play compounds are heavily occupied.

Beatling / Beat Scout screen (preliminary, 2026-07-19):
- Beat Scout: reject/high risk. An exact-name iOS app previously/currently listed as a music livestream discovery utility surfaced. beatscout.com has been registered since 2009 and beatscout.app since 2026. Exact npm names appeared unused, but the same music/app channel collision is enough to deprioritize it.
- Beatling: the more promising candidate. No obvious exact rhythm game, music app, or exact US trademark record surfaced in the quick public-web/index search. beatling.app and the exact npm name appeared open. beatling.com is registered (created 2025-07-28) but currently has no DNS response.
- Beatling residual concern: in the music field, search engines often interpret or redirect the term toward Beatles-related results, and "Beatling" has been used informally for Beatles covers/activities. This creates discoverability and possible similarity concerns that require a proper Beatles/Apple Corps and broader trademark knockout search before adoption.
Conclusion: Beatling merits keeping on the shortlist; Beat Scout does not.

Brand/domain observation: beatl.ing was shown as available for standard registration. The domain hack is memorable and materially strengthens Beatling as a candidate, although domain availability does not resolve the Beatles similarity or trademark questions.

VibeShift / VibeSmith / VibeFlip screen (preliminary, 2026-07-19):
- VibeShift: reject. vibeshift.app is an active Slack tone-rewriting app; an exact VibeShift daily-affirmations iOS app, multiple software/AI companies and projects, and an occupied npm package also surfaced. .com and .app are registered.
- VibeSmith: high risk/reject. Exact active software uses include an AI coding-agent asset/repository manager and a Solana market product at vibesmith.xyz. vibesmith.app carries an exact branded 2025 site. .com and .app are registered. No exact music/rhythm app surfaced, but exact software/app discoverability is already poor.
- VibeFlip: least conflicted of the three, but still weak. No exact music/rhythm app or clear exact trademark record surfaced. However, vibeflip.app is an exact branded 2025 site, VibeFlip is being used for a vibe-coded SaaS marketplace concept/product, and .com/.app/.io are registered. Exact npm appeared open.
- Domain registration dates: vibeshift.com 2017, vibeshift.app 2023; vibesmith.com 2019, vibesmith.app 2025; vibeflip.com 2024, vibeflip.app 2025.
Conclusion: reject VibeShift and VibeSmith. VibeFlip could receive deeper legal screening only if strongly preferred, but its exact domains and emerging software uses make a fresh candidate more practical.

VibeStep screen (preliminary, 2026-07-19):
- Product fit is strong: Vibe can represent casual song play, while Step can identify the beatmap editor as a step sequencer. The name is smooth, family-friendly, and gives the red VIBE / blue STEP toggle a defensible product meaning.
- No exact active App Store, Google Play, Steam, or itch.io product surfaced. Exact npm names appeared unused. vibestep.app and vibestep.dev appeared unregistered.
- Exact prior use exists: a 2018 Devpost/GitHub Android hackathon project named VibeStep used music that changed with running speed. The repository's last commit was 2018-01-21 and its README says StepVibe, suggesting an abandoned prototype, but it is still same-broad-field common-law/search evidence.
- vibestep.com is registered (since 2018) and currently has broken/mismatched HTTPS rather than a verified active product. Other checked exact domains had no DNS, though no-DNS is not proof of availability.
- "Vibestep" also appears as music track/mix titles and Vibe Step is used descriptively for dance/fitness classes. A Chinese Class 25 clothing mark surfaced. No obvious exact US software/game trademark record surfaced in the quick public indexes.
Conclusion: VibeStep is the strongest candidate found in this naming session and merits deeper official trademark/common-law screening. Preliminary risk is medium, not clean, due to the old same-field hackathon app and registered .com, but materially better than VibeShift, VibeSmith, BeatLab, SongLab, or JamLab.

Brand differentiation concern: Beat Saber already uses a strongly associated red/blue dual-color identity and split two-word rhythm-game branding. Colors alone are not necessarily exclusive, but combining a two-word music/game name, neon red/blue palette, and one-color-per-word logo could create avoidable marketplace resemblance or copycat perception. If VibeStep advances, keep red/blue as functional in-app Play/Edit mode colors if desired, but use a distinct primary brand palette and avoid assigning red VIBE / blue STEP in the public wordmark. Formal trade-dress analysis would require counsel.

Brand decision: rebrand Beat Fiend to Vibestep. Initial brand colors are Vibe pink #ff5ea8 and Step lime #c7f464. Icon and textmark assets will be provided separately before implementation. Use the casing "Vibestep" unless the supplied textmark establishes another treatment.

Vibestep rebrand implementation started:
- Central app brand and document title now use Vibestep.
- Removed the legacy Beat Fiend web icon from the rendered brand/favicon while new icon and textmark assets are pending.
- Vibe/play color is #ff5ea8; Step/edit color is #c7f464.
- Primary UI accents now follow the active mode, pink in Play and lime in Edit. Shared backgrounds blend subtle pink/lime glows.
- Quickstart Play/Edit icons use their corresponding mode colors.
- Browser backup/share filenames now use vibestep naming while legacy beat-fiend schemas, storage keys, pairing fragments, old file import support, hosted URL, repository, app ID, and installer artifact name remain intact for compatibility.
- Companion user-facing title, status, controls, package product name, and accents now use Vibestep; its old icon remains excluded from the status page until replacement assets arrive.
Validation: npm test passed 80 app tests plus 13 companion/desktop tests; npm run build passed; npm run lint completed with three pre-existing Fast Refresh warnings; desktop Play/Edit visual checks showed the requested mode colors and no browser runtime errors.

Rebrand boundary hardened:
- `brand.config.js` is now the single runtime source for brand name, Vibe/Step colors, web icon/wordmark, and companion icon/wordmark.
- Web CSS variables, document title/favicon, visible wordmark, export filename slug, companion API name, companion window title/copy, companion HTML branding, and Electron product name derive from this config.
- Electron Builder configuration moved to `electron-builder.config.js` so packaged product naming and optional Windows icon also derive from the shared config.
- Null asset paths intentionally render text/no icon, preventing prior-brand assets from leaking while replacements are pending.
- `BRANDING.md` documents the one-file rebrand process and distinguishes compatibility identifiers that must remain stable.
- Added tests that verify web/companion propagation, reject current brand literals in runtime consumer files, validate colors/slugs/assets, and ensure the shared config is packaged.
Validation: 83 app tests and 13 companion/desktop tests passed; build passed; lint has only three pre-existing Fast Refresh warnings; companion packaging passed and the generated ASAR contains brand.config.js.

title: Fix companion update feed discovery
description: The packaged Windows companion reports 'Unable to find latest version on GitHub' and receives HTTP 406 from https://github.com/Soleone/beatfiend/releases/latest even though companion-v0.1.0 and companion-v0.1.1 release entries exist. Diagnose GitHub Release eligibility and electron-updater tag/version conventions, including whether companion-v* tags are being published as drafts or prereleases or are incompatible with the provider's expected semver tags. Make update checks resolve latest.yml reliably, show a concise user-facing error instead of the full HTTP/feed dump, and verify update discovery from an installed older companion to a newer release.

sources.content: Observed on installed companion 0.1.1: electron-updater GitHubProvider GET /releases/latest returned HTTP 406 and could not select a latest version, while the releases Atom feed listed companion-v0.1.0 and companion-v0.1.1.

title: ux(play): the note trail is confusing players to think they need to hold
description: normally i'd say lets just get rid of the trail and see how it looks. but we now tied the offbeat posiition to the trail length and i dont know where else that info should go. with traditional note scores it is somewhat similar so that was nice. maybe we just live with it and need to tell people about it that there small trails are not about holding, but it'd be unfortunate if it's not intuitive. also without any sort of trail i do wonder if the notes flying in from the right potentially feel less "dynamic", on the other hand it could be less visual noise and improve the experience. maybe we need to try a few things. not sure if you have other ideas how to denote the offbeat position intuitively otherwise. another option could be to just make that trail even thinner or something.

sources.content: normally i'd say lets just get rid of the trail and see how it looks. but we now tied the offbeat posiition to the trail length and i dont know where else that info should go. with traditional note scores it is somewhat similar so that was nice. maybe we just live with it and need to tell people about it that there small trails are not about holding, but it'd be unfortunate if it's not intuitive. also without any sort of trail i do wonder if the notes flying in from the right potentially feel less "dynamic", on the other hand it could be less visual noise and improve the experience. maybe we need to try a few things. not sure if you have other ideas how to denote the offbeat position intuitively otherwise. another option could be to just make that trail even thinner or something.

title: subtle beat like vertical dividers in the play area
description: the goal would be to give the player a better sense of the rhythm and when each note is going to hit, they can gauge how many lines the note has already crossed before it hits the pad. would that mean the lines need to be dynamic based on the bpm?

implementation.approach: BPM-aware static travel guides spaced by half-beat duration relative to projectile travel time
completed: BPM and telegraph timing propagation into the playfield
pure divider spacing helper and focused tests

completion.summary: Added subtle vertical travel guides that adapt their spacing and count to the active BPM. Full beats are slightly stronger than half-beat subdivisions, letting players estimate time to impact without reusing note-hit effects.

title: notes should be "Collected" by the pads
description: maybe it could feel fulfilling if any notes we hit end up in some sort of small container in that lane that ends up getting filled potentially up to 100%. that way a note wouldnt just disapoear after played but have some sort of "permanence" the pad would srt of transform it into some small maybe goo like or just smaller particle that gets storted in some sort of jar with a percentage overlay.

sources.content: maybe it could feel fulfilling if any notes we hit end up in some sort of small container in that lane that ends up getting filled potentially up to 100%. that way a note wouldnt just disapoear after played but have some sort of "permanence" the pad would srt of transform it into some small maybe goo like or just smaller particle that gets storted in some sort of jar with a percentage overlay.

implementation.approach: Track successful hits per lane against map totals, animate captured notes into pad-mounted reservoirs, and show persistent lane percentages
completed: per-lane hit collection state and map totals
pad-mounted fill reservoirs with percentage overlays

completion.summary: Successful notes now become small lane-colored motes that arc into pad-mounted reservoirs. Each reservoir persists its fill and percentage for the run, measured against that lane’s total beatmap notes.

title: make play function work on mobile
description: we have two challenges. 1. adopt to phone screens. not sure if we can affort portrait (would be nice) or require landscape. 2. we need different areas that area clear and large enough to tap consistently. probably spread across left and right side (e.g. kick and snare left). now the real challenge is how we get the audio into mobile. companion seems hard? or how likely do you think it is we can have that? are we reliant on either license free songs hosted on the web app or audio upload into local storage or indexeddb?

sources.content: we have two challenges. 1. adopt to phone screens. not sure if we can affort portrait (would be nice) or require landscape. 2. we need different areas that area clear and large enough to tap consistently. probably spread across left and right side (e.g. kick and snare left)

implementation.approach: Landscape-first fullscreen mobile Play with five multi-touch lane pads and browser-local audio imports
completed: fullscreen hover-none mobile play shell with portrait rotation guidance
split drum and melody touch controls with pointer capture and accessible activation
safe-area-aware transport and edit exit controls
mobile editor return flow with setup panel first
broader mobile audio file selection and IndexedDB persistence request
best-effort storage warning when persistence is denied
secure LAN companion pairing deferred to follow-up sgh

completion.summary: Mobile Play now provides a fullscreen landscape touch surface with five large lane pads. Mobile audio can be selected from local files and stored in IndexedDB without the companion; the app requests persistent storage and warns when storage remains evictable.

description:
for a "run" collect stats on which exact notes where perfect, good and missed. maybe even how much it
was missed. for now we're mainly concerned about the last run, but we want to have the data model prepared to collect all of our runs. and we're actually going to then use that data to visualize in the editor which notes exactly where NOT properly hit and in which direction to help us improve the beatmap, maybe we intuitively play the notes right, but the beatmap was incorrectly set up for certain notes. see where i'm going with this?

title: Migrate beatmap storage to IndexedDB
description: Move browser-owned song packages and beatmaps from localStorage to a versioned IndexedDB repository. Preserve existing libraries through a safe one-time migration, keep current package repository semantics, and validate library export/import compatibility.

title: Aggregate multi-run note feedback
description: Implement revision-aware per-note statistics across persisted Play runs, confidence and direction classification, compact timeline markers, and accessible hover or keyboard details. Follow the linked fresh-session plan.

title: Keep aggregate stats in last-run mode
description: Fix the Run Feedback card regression where enabling 'Show last run on timeline' reduces the card to a single latest-run value instead of retaining the full aggregate context. The switch should affect timeline markers only. The card must continue showing aggregate notes with feedback, repeated issues, consistently early, consistently late, and mixed timing counts across all revision-compatible persisted Play runs. Consider adding one or two dedicated Latest run comparison rows beneath the stable aggregate rows so the player can compare the newest run with historical per-note trends. Useful candidates include latest-run perfect/good/needs-work counts; early/on-time/late note counts; and note-level improved/same/regressed counts versus each note's aggregate tendency. Do not introduce a single global average timing offset across unrelated notes, since that can hide opposing errors. The latest-run rows should supplement, never replace, the aggregate statistics. Keep the card description, aggregate metric rows, explanatory copy, and control positions stable so toggling causes no layout shift. Preserve outcome colors on last-run timeline markers; confidence should use ring strength and attempt counts rather than muting markers. Relevant files: src/game/use-play-run-history.ts, src/editor/RunFeedbackSummaryCard.tsx, src/editor/RunFeedbackMarker.tsx, and src/App.css. Validate aggregate card values remain unchanged before and after toggling, timeline marker data switches to the latest run, and any comparison rows accurately use current-revision note evidence.

sources.content: Session decision: last-run mode is a timeline inspection mode, not a replacement for aggregate card statistics. The aggregate card provides context while latest-run markers show immediate performance.

title: design secure mobile companion pairing
description: Explore an opt-in phone-to-desktop companion mode using LAN discovery or QR pairing. Require exact-origin CORS, short-lived authenticated pairing, local-network permission handling, and direct file import fallback. Keep this separate from the first mobile Play release because phone loopback cannot reach the desktop and Safari support is uncertain.

title: attach local audio to an existing song package
description: Allow an imported shared beatmap or existing song package with unavailable audio to attach a user-selected local audio file in browser IndexedDB while preserving the package ID, maps, timing, and source metadata.

implementation.approach: Attach a user-selected local audio Blob to an existing song package through a focused browser-audio attachment hook and IndexedDB association
completed: attach action names the target shared song
package ID, beatmaps, timing profiles, and YouTube source metadata remain unchanged
browser audio association replaces prior browser audio with rollback cleanup
duration mismatch confirmation before replacing audio
pending guard prevents concurrent attachment writes

completion.summary: Existing shared beatmaps can now attach matching local audio on mobile without creating a separate blank song package.
validation: blob audio playback and reload persistence
